laws.title: Public Law 7-42 | Transportation Act of 2022
laws.status_basis: The source is a generally applicable transportation enactment that was later repealed externally. Its active provisions were real while operative: it creates a Bus Rapid Transit Division, the Federal Transit Administration, and the Transportation Security Administration, then adds municipal-law restrictions and a repeal of the prior towing act. Because later law repealed it in full, the correct disposition is statutory history only.

provisions.text_summary: Creates a Bus Rapid Transit Division under the Department of Transportation and sets nationwide transit duties.
provisions.exact_change: Retain only as historical note material; no live Title 49 text should be created from a repealed enactment.
provisions.evidence: There shall be a Bus Rapid Transit Division under the Federal Government.
provisions.notes: General and permanent while active, but fully repealed now.

provisions.text_summary: Creates the Federal Transit Administration and assigns transit-routing duties.
provisions.exact_change: Keep as history-only; do not codify a new permanent section for a repealed act.
provisions.evidence: The Federal Transit Administration is hereby formed under the direction of the Department of Transportation.

provisions.text_summary: Creates the Transportation Security Administration and gives it transit-security and air-traffic-related duties.
provisions.exact_change: History note only; no present Code text is required after repeal.
provisions.evidence: The Transportation Security Administration is hereby established under the direction of the Department of Transportation.

provisions.text_summary: Bars municipalities from regulating public transit or towing services and repeals conflicting municipal laws.
provisions.exact_change: Record the municipal-law repeal as history only; do not invent a federal Code amendment.
provisions.evidence: Any municipal laws not specifically mentioned herein that regulate such are hereby repealed and overruled.
provisions.notes: The clause references prior municipal law, not a numbered U.S. Code section.

provisions.text_summary: Repeals the prior 'S 63 Towing Companies Regulation Act'.
provisions.target: prior S. 63 towing act; non-U.S. Code target
provisions.exact_change: Keep the repeal cross-reference as history only.
provisions.evidence: S 63 Towing Companies Regulation Act is hereby struck down and repealed.

provisions.text_summary: Non-binding development suggestions about buses and gun-free zones.
provisions.exact_change: Do not codify; this text expressly says it is not law of the United States.
provisions.evidence: This section should not be construed to be the law of the United States.

current_implementation.detail: The history-note treatment is legally sufficient, but the XML note carries full quotedContent and archive-link baggage that should be cleaned up if the branch keeps the note.

recommended_actions: Retain as statutory history only at the closest Title 49 anchor.
Remove any unnecessary quotedContent dump and archive-link clutter from the note if present.
Do not create a new permanent Code section.

laws.title: Public Law 7-43 | District of Columbia Reorganization Initiative Act
laws.status_basis: This is a local District of Columbia charter amendment, not a permanent U.S. Code enactment. It changes the charter's council structure, abolishes a charter section, and sets a mayoral succession order. The later record shows the charter itself was repealed, so this enactment belongs in local history only.

provisions.text_summary: Reduces the D.C. council from six members to five and abolishes Article I, Section IV of the charter.
provisions.exact_change: Treat as local charter history; no federal Code amendment is appropriate.
provisions.evidence: The Council established shall consist of five members elected on a partisan basis.

provisions.text_summary: Rewrites the mayoral designation rule in Article II of the charter.
provisions.exact_change: Local charter text only; keep as historical note material.
provisions.evidence: The Mayor may designate the officer or officers of the executive department of the District, with the consent of the City Council.

provisions.text_summary: Establishes a line of succession for the D.C. mayor's office.
provisions.target: D.C. City Charter / local succession rule
provisions.exact_change: Do not map to permanent U.S. Code text; this is a local governance rule.
provisions.evidence: The Line of Succession for the Office of the Mayor of Washington, DC shall be the following:

current_implementation.detail: The branch note is directionally right in treating this as local history, but it should remain history-only and not be treated as live federal Code text.

recommended_actions: Keep only a local historical note; do not create U.S. Code text.
Treat the later charter repeal as superseding history.
Do not promote any section of this act into permanent positive law.

laws.title: Public Law 7-44 | The Bye Bye Terrorism Act
laws.status_basis: The source is a permanent general firearms-policy enactment that limits fully automatic firearms to semi-automatic status. It is not temporary on its face, but later repeal removed it from live law. A history note is sufficient now.

provisions.text_summary: Defines and reclassifies fully automatic firearms within the firearms code framework.
provisions.exact_change: History note only; no current Code text should be created from a repealed firearms policy law.
provisions.evidence: Any fully automatic firearms shall be classified as semi-automatic firearms.

provisions.text_summary: Contains the effective-date rule for the firearms policy.
provisions.exact_change: Keep as a historical effective-date reference only.
provisions.evidence: This Act shall go into effect immediately upon passage.

current_implementation.detail: The enactment is legally history-only now, but the note should be kept concise and not treated as active text.

recommended_actions: Retain as historical note only at the Title 18 firearms anchor.
No new Code section is required because the law is fully repealed.
Keep the note concise and history-only.

laws.title: Public Law 7-45 | United States Capitol Police Reorganization Act of 2022
laws.status_basis: This is a Capitol Police reorganization measure with local/Congressional subject matter. It repeals part of the earlier Capitol Police establishment law, creates a new board structure, and governs deployment outside the Capitol jurisdiction. Later repeal leaves only a historical note role.

provisions.text_summary: Repeals portions of the prior Capitol Police law and creates a new Capitol Police Board structure.
provisions.exact_change: Record as local/Congressional history only; no permanent federal Code section is required.
provisions.evidence: The following shall be members of the Capitol Police Board.

provisions.text_summary: Sets Capitol Police protective authority and arrest powers.
provisions.exact_change: Keep as history only because the enactment is fully repealed.
provisions.evidence: The United States Capitol Police is authorized to protect, in any area of the United States, the person of any Member of Congress.

provisions.text_summary: Restricts deployment outside the Capitol Police jurisdiction except for emergencies and protective services.
provisions.exact_change: History note only; do not create live Code text.
provisions.evidence: The Chief of the Capitol Police may not deploy any officer outside of the areas established by law for the jurisdiction of the Capitol Police unless...

current_implementation.detail: This belongs in historical/local-note form only. Any live Code placement would overstate its current force because the law was later repealed.

recommended_actions: Keep only as historical note material at the closest Congressional/Capitol anchor.
Do not write a permanent U.S. Code section.
Preserve the repeal chronology in the history note.

laws.title: Public Law 7-46 | Whistleblower Protection Act
laws.status_basis: The supplied source is not recoverable in the record available to this review. The attached law.html is a permission-denied page, so the enacted statutory text cannot be lawfully reconstructed. No operative conclusions beyond source unavailability can be drawn.

current_implementation.detail: No recoverable statutory text was available in the supplied source files, so there is nothing lawful to codify from the review record.

recommended_actions: Leave unresolved and do not invent operative text.
If recovery later becomes possible, add only a source-limited historical note.
Do not assign a live Code section from the current record.

laws.title: Public Law 7-47 | The Federal Bar Association Act of 2022
laws.status_basis: This was a generally applicable, permanent bar-governance enactment that established the Federal Bar Association, a bar-exam structure, and a code of conduct. A later enactment expressly repealed it, so the current disposition is historical note only.

provisions.text_summary: Creates the Federal Bar Association structure and membership/administration rules.
provisions.exact_change: Retain as history only because the law was later repealed in full.
provisions.evidence: There shall be established a Federal Bar Association.

provisions.text_summary: Creates the bar-exam and admission framework tied to the Federal Bar Association.
provisions.exact_change: No active Code section is warranted after repeal.
provisions.evidence: The Federal Bar Association shall have the power to administer the bar exam.

provisions.text_summary: Sets the code of conduct for lawyers admitted under the act.
provisions.exact_change: History note only.
provisions.evidence: Code of Conduct.

current_implementation.detail: The note is directionally correct as history, but the branch still treats a repealed organic bar law as if it were live subject-matter text.

recommended_actions: Keep as a repealed-history note only.
Do not leave any live Title 28 text in place for this enactment.
Preserve the later repeal chronology in the note.

laws.title: Public Law 7-48 | OCJCS Reform Act of 2022
laws.status_basis: The source is a permanent, generally applicable military reorganization. It abolishes most combatant-command structures, reallocates duties to the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs, and is later repealed by a subsequent enactment. That makes history-note treatment sufficient now.

provisions.text_summary: Abolishes or restructures combatant commands except for the specified exception and reassigns duties.
provisions.exact_change: History only; the later repeal means no live Title 10 section should remain from this act.
provisions.evidence: All combatant commanders shall be abolished, except for USSOCOM.

provisions.text_summary: Later enactment repeals this law and restores/reworks the command structure.
provisions.exact_change: Record the repeal chronology only.
provisions.evidence: The later record repeals Public Law 7-48.

current_implementation.detail: The act should be retained as history only; the branch should not leave behind live military-organic text once the repeal is accounted for.

recommended_actions: Retain as historical note material at the Title 10 anchor.
Do not create a new Code section for a repealed reform act.
Keep the repeal chronology explicit.

laws.title: Public Law 7-49 | Legislative Initiative to Grow Monetary Access Act of 2022 (LIGMA Act)
laws.status_basis: This enactment is an ongoing compensation rule while active: it gives House and Senate teams recurring in-game paychecks and applies to successor games. It is now fully repealed, so the only legally sufficient treatment is statutory history.

provisions.text_summary: Provides in-game compensation for elected legislative officials.
provisions.exact_change: History note only; no live spending or pay section should be created.
provisions.evidence: This act grants in-game compensation to elected federal legislative officials.

provisions.text_summary: Sets the effective date and the recurring paycheck cadence, including successor games.
provisions.exact_change: Keep as repealed-history only.
provisions.evidence: The timeline for this effect is specified in section 4.

current_implementation.detail: The current note is legally adequate as history, but it should remain clearly historical because the law no longer has operative force.

recommended_actions: Keep the law only as historical note material.
Do not create a live Title 31 compensation section.
Preserve its repeal status in the note text.

laws.title: Public Law 7-50 | Coast Guard Base Repurposing Act of 2022
laws.status_basis: This is a site-specific property and jurisdiction repurposing law about a named Coast Guard base in Washington, D.C. Remastered. It is local/property-specific rather than permanent Code text, and later repeal makes a history note sufficient.

provisions.text_summary: Defines the base, repurposes it to the Navy, and changes jurisdiction/signage.
provisions.exact_change: Treat as site-specific history only; do not create permanent positive-law text.
provisions.evidence: The Potomac River Coast Guard Base shall be repurposed to service the United States Navy.

provisions.text_summary: States the factual purpose for the repurposing decision.
provisions.exact_change: Keep as explanatory history only.
provisions.evidence: There is no reason to have a Coast Guard installation at Washington, D.C.

current_implementation.detail: The note is legally sufficient as a history note; no Code section is warranted because the law is site-specific and later repealed.

recommended_actions: Keep as history only at the Title 14 anchor.
Do not create permanent U.S. Code text for a repurposed installation.
Maintain the repeal chronology in the note.

laws.title: Public Law 7-51 | Federal Agent Law Training Mandate
laws.status_basis: The source was deleted or not found. The only recovered material is a source-limited record indicating the law could not be recovered, so the statutory text cannot be reconstructed lawfully. The proper disposition is unresolved source-limited history only.

current_implementation.detail: The existing note is a lawful source-limited history note; no operative language is invented, which is exactly what the record permits.

recommended_actions: Retain only as a source-limited historical note.
Do not infer any operative provisions from the missing source.
No Code section should be created from the current record.
